steps.title: Connect pressure to avoidance
steps.content: Large vortices produce greater snout pressure fluctuations for narrow-headed fish than for wide-headed fish. The second team's hypothesis says greater pressure fluctuations help fish avoid obstructions in low visibility. Combining those ideas, narrow-headed fish should have fewer collisions, while wide-headed fish should bump into obstructions more often under the same large-vortex, low-visibility conditions.

steps.title: Reject missing comparisons
steps.content: Choice A compares only narrow-headed fish with other narrow-headed fish, so it does not test the head-shape difference. Choice B says narrow- and wide-headed fish collide equally often, which would not support greater pressure fluctuations improving avoidance. Choice D gives only the wide-headed fish's collision rate and no comparison to narrow-headed fish, so it cannot show whether lower fluctuations mean poorer avoidance. The remaining finding shows the wide-headed fish, with lower pressure fluctuations, bumping into obstructions more often than the narrow-headed fish, with higher pressure fluctuations. The answer is C.
